summary: My name is Shinichi Seike, born on January 5th, 1888 in Doi-mura, Ehime Prefecture. Despite my family's poverty, my mother insisted on sending me to Yahata Commercial High School. After struggling to obtain a visa to America, I joined the Rikko-Kai in Tokyo and was baptized before emigrating to the U.S. in 1908. I became the chairman of our group on the ship Iyo-Maru and worked hard to spread Christianity.

I was born in Doi-mura, Higashiuba Gun, Ehime Prefecture, a rural area where life was peaceful but poverty was common. My family lived there during my childhood, and despite hardships, my mother was determined to educate me. It was a small village far from the city, which made opportunities like going to high school or emigrating to America quite difficult.

Ehime Prefecture is where I was born and raised, specifically in Doi-mura. It was a rural and poor area in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. My family struggled financially, and it was rare for children from such places to attend high school. Later, a Diet member from Ehime and the prefectural governor helped me with my passport application to go to America.

At age 16, I started attending Yahata Commercial High School, which was about 2.5 miles from my home. It was unusual for poor families like mine to send their children to high school, but my mother insisted despite our financial difficulties. I studied there for four years and graduated, though job opportunities were scarce in our rural area afterward.

Tacoma was mentioned as a point of reference for distance when I talked about how far Yahata Commercial High School was from my home. It was about the same distance as from my village to Tacoma, which showed how remote my home was. I didn’t live in Tacoma, but it helped me understand the challenges of traveling far for education or immigration.

Many Japanese immigrants in Seattle came from the Yahata area, which is near where I lived. When I finally came to America in 1908, I landed in Seattle directly with a group from the Rikko-Kai. Seattle was a common destination for Japanese immigrants seeking new opportunities in the U.S.

Kiryu was the family register I used to try to obtain a visa to America, as my friend Kiryu suggested it might help. I put my name on their family register hoping it would increase my chances, but unfortunately, it did not work. This was part of my early struggles to get permission to emigrate.

When I couldn't get a visa to America, I applied for an immigrant visa to Hawaii, which was popular at the time. However, the local policeman investigating my background did not approve my application. This was another setback in my efforts to emigrate from Japan.

My English teacher at Yahata Commercial High School was Mr. Ouchi, who was originally from Sendai. Later, he became the manager of the Rikko-Kai Association in Tokyo, where I sought help to go to America. Sendai was significant because both Ouchi and the Rikko-Kai president, Kihei Shimazaki, were from there.

Mr. Ouchi was my English teacher from Sendai when I attended Yahata Commercial High School. Later, he became the manager of the Rikko-Kai Association in Tokyo, where I boarded at his house while preparing to emigrate. He was a key figure who supported me during my time in Tokyo and helped me connect with the Rikko-Kai community.

Rikko-Kai was an association in Tokyo dedicated to educating and sending Japanese youth overseas, especially to America and South America. Its president, Kihei Shimazaki, was a spirited Christian leader who built a large hall and emphasized Christian education. I stayed with the Rikko-Kai for about one and a half to two years, attending classes, receiving spiritual guidance, and preparing for emigration.

Kihei Shimazaki was the president of the Rikko-Kai and a great Christian leader from Sendai. Despite suffering from tuberculosis and financial difficulties, he built a large hall to educate youth and send them overseas. He personally encouraged me during interviews and inspired many young people, including myself, with his faith and dedication.

Gunpei Yamamura was mentioned as a man of equal stature in Japan to Kihei Shimazaki, the president of Rikko-Kai. While I did not have direct interaction with Yamamura, his reputation helped me understand the significance of Shimazaki’s work in Christian education and immigration.

I moved to Tokyo to seek help from Mr. Ouchi and join the Rikko-Kai Association. Tokyo was where I boarded, studied, and prepared to emigrate to America. It was also where I enrolled at Meiji University to meet passport requirements and where I met influential people like Diet member Watanabe and others who helped me.

Nihon Kirisuto Kyodan, or the Japanese United Church of Christ, was the denomination of the church where Mr. Shimazaki was a minister. I was baptized by Mr. Shimazaki at the Nihon Church of Christ in Kanda, Tokyo, before leaving for America. The church played an important role in my Christian education and spiritual preparation.

The Diet of Japan was significant because a member named Mr. Watanabe, who was from my prefecture, helped me obtain my passport. When the Diet was in session in Tokyo, I sought out Mr. Watanabe and explained my desire to go to America. His support, along with others, was crucial in meeting the strict requirements for emigration at that time.

Mr. Watanabe was a Diet member from my prefecture who helped me tremendously by becoming my guarantor for my passport application. I met him in Tokyo early one morning and explained my plans to go to America. His support was vital in overcoming the strict immigration controls of the time.

The governor of Ehime Prefecture was known to Mr. Watanabe and was part of the network that helped me get my passport. My high school mate was a secretary to the governor, Mr. Saionji, who received a letter from Watanabe recommending my passport application. This connection helped facilitate my emigration process.

Saionji was the secretary to the governor of Ehime Prefecture and a high school mate of mine. When Mr. Watanabe wrote to him recommending my passport application, Saionji helped process it. This connection was important in securing my permission to leave Japan for America.

I enrolled at Meiji University in Tokyo to meet the strict requirements for obtaining a passport to America, as students were allowed to travel more easily. Although I did not live in the university dorms, I arranged to appear as if I was boarding at an influential elder's house connected to the university. This strategic enrollment helped me secure my passport in less than a month.

Betania Home was one of the boarding places in Tokyo where youth from the Rikko-Kai stayed. It housed about 5 to 8 people who either cooked for themselves or had meals prepared by the landlady. While I personally boarded at Mr. Ouchi's house, Betania Home was part of the community supporting immigrants preparing to go abroad.

Iyo-Maru was the small ship that carried our group of 16 people from Japan to America in April 1908. The voyage took about 17 to 18 days, during which we held Christian services on deck, singing hymns and spreading our faith. I was the oldest in the group and served as chairman, leading our spiritual activities on the journey.

Kanda in Tokyo was where I attended church services regularly before leaving Japan. It was also the location of the Nihon Church of Christ where I was baptized by Mr. Shimazaki. Kanda was a spiritual center for me during my preparation to emigrate to America.

The Rikko-Kai members, including myself, used to preach Christianity in a manner similar to the Salvation Army, beating drums and carrying lanterns. Sometimes we faced hostility, with people throwing stones at us, which forced us to retreat for safety. This experience showed the challenges we faced in spreading our faith.

While not directly mentioned by name, the immigration authorities were very strict about issuing passports and visas to America. They required a guarantor in the U.S. and conducted thorough investigations. Thanks to the support of guarantors like Mr. Watanabe and Mr. Shiba, I was able to meet their conditions and obtain my passport.

Yoshioka was a friend whose father, Mr. Shiba, a wealthy wine dealer, became one of my guarantors for my passport application. This support was crucial in fulfilling the strict conditions required to emigrate to America. Yoshioka and his family played an important role in helping me achieve my goal.
